q: The 4 rules for prioritization include all EXCEPT:
options: The more vital the organ, the higher the priority
The youngest patient always comes first
rationale: The 4 rules: (1) Acute beats Chronic, (2) Unstable beats Stable, (3) Actual beats Potential, (4) The more vital the organ, the higher the priority. Age is not one of the rules.

q: Among the following patients, which has the HIGHEST priority: COPD, CHF, or acute appendicitis?
rationale: Rule 1: Acute beats Chronic. COPD and CHF are chronic conditions. Acute appendicitis is an acute condition and therefore has the highest priority.

q: A patient described as 'newly admitted less than 24 hours' would be considered:
rationale: Unstable descriptors: newly diagnosed, newly admitted, not ready for discharge, admitted <24 hours, changing assessment, unexpected S/Sx. Stable: ready for discharge, admitted >24 hours, unchanged assessment.

q: Using the vital organ prioritization rule, which organ has the HIGHEST priority?
rationale: Vital organ priority: (1) Brain, (2) Lung, (3) Heart, (4) Liver, (5) Kidney, (6) Pancreas. Use this rule with the organ of the MODIFYING PHRASE, not the diagnosis.

q: Which tasks can an LPN/LVN perform?
options: Stable, predictable tasks and monitoring established plans
Initial assessments and care plan development
Teaching new diagnoses and evaluating outcomes
rationale: LPNs handle stable, predictable tasks and can monitor patients on established care plans. They CANNOT do admission, discharge, transfer, or first assessments after a change.

q: An LPN reports hearing new crackles on a patient. Who should assess this patient?
options: The LPN can assess since they heard it
The RN must assess since this is a new/changed symptom
rationale: The RN must assess the patient since this is a new onset or change of symptoms. LPNs are NOT allowed to assess: admission, discharge, transfer, or first assessment after a change.

q: When handling inappropriate behavior from staff, which response is ALWAYS wrong?
options: Confront them and take over immediately
rationale: Never ignore inappropriate behaviors. The 4 typical answers: (1) Tell supervisor, (2) Confront/take over immediately, (3) Talk later, (4) Ignore. Ignoring is always wrong - use it as a teaching moment.

q: Piaget's Concrete Operations stage (7-11 years) is characterized by children who are:
options: Rule-oriented with one right way to do things
rationale: Concrete Operations (7-11): '7/11 Grocery Stores are surrounded by concrete.' These children are rule-oriented - live and die by the rules. Cannot abstract. One way to do things, everything else is wrong.

q: When teaching a preschooler (3-6 years) about a procedure, the nurse should:
options: Teach a week in advance for thorough preparation
Teach 2 hours before, the morning of, or the day of the procedure
Teach using abstract concepts and diagrams
rationale: Preschoolers are in the 'imaginative' stage. Teach 2 hours before, morning of, or day of the procedure in future tense. Don't give them time to imagine the worst. They can learn by playing.

q: A modifying phrase that changes priority is exemplified by which scenario?
options: An MI patient is always highest priority regardless
A patient with angina and unstable BP is higher priority than a stable MI
Chronic conditions are always highest priority
rationale: Without modifying phrases, go by diagnosis (MI > Angina). WITH modifying phrases: 'unstable BP and Angina' is higher priority than 'stable vital signs and MI.' Unstable beats Stable.

q: Concrete Operations children (7-11) should be taught about procedures:
options: A day or two ahead of time
rationale: Teach Concrete Operations children (7-11) a day or two ahead of time. They are rule-oriented and need to know the exact steps. Use present tense and demonstrate on a doll or model.

q: Seven principles for psychiatric nursing tests include: don't give advice. If a patient asks 'What should I do?', the best response is:
options: Tell them exactly what to do
'What do you think you should do?'
rationale: In psychiatric nursing: Don't give advice. If patient says 'What do you think I should do?' - reply by asking them the same question: 'What do you think you should do?'

q: The pharmacology guessing strategy states: if you know what a drug does but don't know the side effect, pick:
options: The side effect in the same body system the drug works in
rationale: If you know what a drug does but don't know the S/E, pick the S/E in the same body system. GI drug → diarrhea. Cardiac drug → tachycardia. CNS drug → drowsiness.

q: When choosing between two patients and one has 'expected' symptoms while the other has 'unexpected' symptoms, who is the priority?
rationale: Experiencing unexpected S/Sx makes a patient unstable and therefore higher priority than one with expected symptoms of their disease. Expected = stable, Unexpected = unstable.

q: Piaget's Sensorimotor stage (0-2 years) means children:
options: Only think about what they are sensing now
rationale: Sensorimotor (0-2 years): children only think about what they are currently sensing/experiencing. Object permanence develops during this stage.

q: In psychiatric nursing, why should you NOT accept gifts from patients?
options: What seems like a simple gift may have different meaning to the patient
rationale: Don't give/accept gifts in psych. If a schizophrenic patient gives you flowers, to the HCP it may be flowers; to the patient, it may be a marriage proposal. The meaning can be very different.

q: An LPN can take a post-op patient out of bed ONLY after:
options: The RN has done so first
rationale: LPN can take patient out of bed post-op ONLY after the nurse (RN) has first done so. LPN can also take V/S post-op only after the RN has first done so.

q: K-wasting diuretics can be identified by:
rationale: Any diuretic ending in 'X' wastes potassium (X-es out K) like Lasix, PLUS Diuril (a thiazide). K-sparing diuretics: Spironolactone, Triamterene.

q: Baclofen is a muscle relaxant. The memory aid for this is:
options: 'When you are on baclofen, you are on your back loafing'
rationale: 'When you are on baclofen, you are on your back loafing' helps remember it's a muscle relaxant. Teach patients: don't drink, don't drive, don't operate heavy machinery.

q: The 'worst case scenario' test-taking strategy works by:
options: Always choosing the most dramatic answer
Asking 'What would happen if I did NOT do this?' and picking the one with worst consequences
Choosing the answer with the most medical terminology
rationale: Ask: 'What would happen if I did not implement this task?' The answer where NOT doing it leads to the worst consequence (like DEATH) is the correct priority answer.
